turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're diving into AI privacy—a tension I find fascinating. These systems are hungry for our data, but what happens when that data includes our personal information? Can you help us understand the stakes here?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: The stakes are surprisingly direct. These models don't just learn patterns—they memorize. A 2021 study showed GPT-2 could be coaxed into regurgitating real names, phone numbers, and addresses from its training data. Your personal information isn't just fuel; it can be extracted back out.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's unsettling—so these systems can essentially be tricked into giving back what we put in. How exactly do these extraction attacks work, and is this problem getting worse as models grow larger?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Extraction attacks use carefully crafted prompts to coax out memorized content—almost like interrogating a witness with leading questions. And yes, it's getting worse: research shows larger models have greater memorization capacity, and sensitive data can be extracted even if it appeared only a few times in training.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So extraction attacks get the data out directly—but I understand there are sneakier approaches too, inference attacks that don't need the model to spit out your actual information. How do those work?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of model inversion attacks like reconstructing a face from a sketch artist's notes. Researchers demonstrated that with just a facial recognition model's confidence scores, they could reconstruct recognizable images of actual people in the training set—without ever seeing the original photos.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's chilling—reconstructing someone's face from confidence scores alone. And I know there's another inference attack type too—membership inference. Can you explain what that reveals and why it matters?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Membership inference asks a simple but dangerous question: was your data used to train this model? If a medical AI confirms your health records were in its training set, that alone reveals you likely have that condition. It's not extracting the data—it's confirming your association with it, which can be just as damaging.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So between memorization, inversion, and membership attacks, it feels like we're fundamentally exposed. Before we wrap up, what gives you hope—is there a path forward where AI capability and

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: privacy coexist? Yes—the tension is driving real innovation. GDPR forced us to confront that you can't surgically remove someone's data from a trained neural network—information is distributed, entangled. That uncomfortable truth is now catalyzing technical solutions and a renegotiation of the social contract between developers and individuals.
